data.content_ref: inline:The target's mechanism claims the repository's hard-set data show 'fixed precision 0.125 and recall 1.0 yields zero net benefit because the critic flags everything, so filtering is a no-op on the already-correct majority.' But recall 1.0 with precision 0.125 does NOT mean the critic 'flags everything' — it means the critic catches all wrong candidates but also flags many correct ones (7 false positives for every 1 true positive). The target conflates 'recall=1.0' with 'flags everything,' then uses this conflation to argue the failure mode is 'inability to discriminate which veto signals are reliable.' The actual repository data say net fixed-minus-broke = 0 because 4/5 majorities were already right (base error 0.01), so there were no wrong-majority questions to fix — not because the critic indiscriminately flagged everything. The target's causal diagnosis ('flags everything → no-op') is a misreading of the measured precision/recall decomposition, and the proposed calibrated-confidence remedy is solving a discrimination problem that the data do not exhibit. The real failure is absence of fixable-wrong-majorities (a base-rate problem), not a threshold-discrimination problem.
